summary: High‑energy second set, flawless Promised Land comeback, stellar keyboards
blurb: The show opens with a solid Jack Straw and builds into an electrifying second set that survives a brief power outage. Highlights include a perfect Promised Land restart, fiery Brent on We Can Run, and a soaring The Other One jam. Jerry’s tone is spot‑on and the rhythm section drives the band throughout.
review: July 10, 1990 at Carter‑Finley Stadium delivered one of those storm‑charged nights that Deadheads still talk about. After a tight first set anchored by Jack Straw, Loser and an upbeat Bird Song, the band dove into a second set that turned a brief power outage into legend – they slipped right back into The Promised Land without missing a beat. The sequence from Uncle John’s Band through a blistering Playing In The Band jam was pure joy, and the ensuing Drums‑Space‑The Other One stretch roared with crisp, locked‑in drumming and one of the most inspired post‑1975 renditions of The Other One. Brent Mydland’s keyboards were on fire, especially on We Can Run, while his occasional accordion adds a quirky Hornsby flavor that many fans found a fun twist. Jerry’s guitar tone was described as “perfect,” his vocal delivery spot‑on, and the rhythm section – Phil’s steady bass, Mickey and Bill’s energetic percussion – kept the momentum rolling to a heartfelt Brokedown Palace encore.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Eight of ten recordings are overwhelmingly positive, repeatedly praising the second‑set energy, the seamless Promised Land restart, Brent's fiery keyboards and the crisp drums/space jam. No consistent negative comments appear, so a strong positive adjustment is warranted.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture with crystal‑clear, perfect audio; no drop‑outs despite the brief power failure, and it includes the full show flow.

key_highlights: Second set survives a power outage and comes back stronger than ever
Uncle John's Band and The Promised Land performances are flawless
Brent Mydland’s fiery keyboards dominate We Can Run and add accordion color
The Other One jam with drums/space is a standout, storm‑enhanced improvisation

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar tone is described as perfect and his vocal delivery spot‑on, especially on Uncle John’s Band and the post‑outage Promised Land restart. He grins, looks excited, and keeps the band locked in.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides a solid, steady bass foundation throughout; reviewers note he stayed powered up during the lightning strike and kept the groove tight.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s nasal twang on Big River earns praise, and his overall vibe adds to the night‑time atmosphere, especially during the first set jams.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland shines with fiery keyboards on We Can Run and throughout the second set; his occasional accordion adds a quirky Hornsby flavor that most fans found fun rather than intrusive.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart’s drumming is crisp, energetic, and drives the powerful fourth‑quarter sequences, contributing to the tight drum jams noted by listeners.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann locks in with Hart for a locked‑in, storm‑enhanced rhythm section; his work on the drums/space segment is highlighted as unusually noteworthy.
